content.text: A recurring report is a document that is generated on a fixed cadence — daily, weekly, or monthly — from a consistent set of sources with a consistent structure. Cowork handles recurring reports as scheduled tasks: the goal is written once, the schedule fires automatically, and the workspace accumulates a history of outputs. The combination of predictable structure, fresh connector data, and persistent workspace state makes Cowork particularly effective for this use case.
Architecture of a Recurring Report Task

attrs.alt: Recurring report architecture: schedule trigger flows through data sources and task execution to produce timestamped outputs in the shared workspace

content.text: Every recurring report task combines four elements: a schedule trigger (daily, weekly, or monthly), data sources (connectors plus workspace files), task execution logic (the goal), and a set of outputs. The workspace is the persistence layer — state files track watermarks between runs, prior outputs enable period-over-period comparison, and a run log records every execution. This structure enables a report to get smarter over time as the workspace accumulates history.
Goal Design for Recurring Reports

The goal for a recurring report must be written to produce correct output on every run, including the first run, runs where data is missing, and runs after a gap (a missed schedule cycle). Three design patterns address these requirements.
Pattern 1: Stateless Snapshot
Each run produces a fresh snapshot of the current state with no reference to prior runs. Simple and robust — appropriate when the audience only needs the current picture, not a trend.

## Daily Support Queue Report

Fetch all open Jira tickets in project SUPPORT (connector: Jira).
Group by: status (Open, In Progress, Waiting on Customer, Escalated).
For each group, list: ticket count, oldest ticket age (days), tickets marked Critical.

Output a markdown table:
| Status | Count | Oldest (days) | Critical |

Also list any ticket open longer than 14 days (by key and title).

Output to /workspace/reports/support-queue.md (overwrite each run)
Also write /workspace/reports/support-queue-{YYYY-MM-DD}.md (date-stamped archive)

Append one line to /workspace/logs/run-log.md:
'Support queue report: {date}, {N} open tickets, {M} critical'
Pattern 2: Period-over-Period Comparison
Each run reads the prior period's output from /workspace/ and computes deltas. Enables trend tracking without a database — the workspace accumulates the history.

## Weekly Revenue Report

Step 1: Read /workspace/reports/weekly-revenue-latest.md.
If the file does not exist, this is the first run — skip comparison, note 'Baseline run'.

Step 2: Fetch this week's revenue data from Google Analytics
(connector: Google Analytics, metrics: revenue, transactions, avg_order_value,
date_range: last 7 days, dimension: none).

Step 3: Compute:
- This week's total revenue and transaction count
- WoW change vs prior run data (if available)
- Rolling 4-week average (if 4+ prior reports exist in /workspace/reports/)

Step 4: Flag any week with WoW revenue change > 15% in either direction.

Step 5: Write report to:
  /workspace/reports/weekly-revenue-latest.md (overwrite — current period data only)
  /workspace/reports/weekly-revenue-{YYYY-MM-DD}.md (archive)

Step 6: Append one line to /workspace/logs/run-log.md
Pattern 3: Incremental Accumulation
Each run processes only new records since the last run, appending to a cumulative output. Appropriate for audit logs, event trackers, and datasets that grow continuously.

## Daily New Customer Report

Step 1: Read /workspace/state/last_customer_id.txt.
If not found, use ID 0.

Step 2: Fetch new customers with ID > {last_customer_id} from CRM
(connector: CRM, filter: id > last_customer_id, order: id asc).

If no new customers: append '{date}: no new customers' to /workspace/logs/run-log.md and stop.

Step 3: For each new customer, extract:
id, name, plan, mrr, acquisition_channel, sign-up date

Step 4: Append new rows to /workspace/reports/all-customers.csv
(append — do not overwrite; create file if not exists)

Step 5: Write /workspace/reports/new-customers-{YYYY-MM-DD}.md
with today's new customers and a summary (count, total new MRR)

Step 6: Overwrite /workspace/state/last_customer_id.txt with the highest ID seen.
Report Templates in the Workspace

For reports with a fixed visual structure (headers, sections, table layouts), store a template in /workspace/templates/ and instruct the engine to use it as the structural guide. This ensures consistent formatting across runs even as the underlying data changes.

# Goal pattern using a template:
Read /workspace/templates/weekly-ops-template.md for the required report structure.

Fetch this week's data [sources block].

Produce the report by filling in the template sections:
- Replace {{OPEN_TICKETS}} with the current count
- Replace {{RESOLVED_THIS_WEEK}} with the count of tickets closed in last 7 days
- Replace {{P1_INCIDENTS}} with a bulleted list of P1 incidents
- Replace {{METRICS_TABLE}} with the computed metrics table

Do not add sections not in the template.
Do not remove sections even if data is empty — write 'None this week' as the value.

Output to /workspace/reports/ops-report-{YYYY-MM-DD}.md
Anomaly Detection in Reports

A recurring report becomes significantly more valuable when it flags anomalies — values outside expected ranges — rather than just reporting current numbers. Building anomaly detection into the goal eliminates the need for the reader to scan for problems.

# Anomaly detection instructions (add to any metric report goal):

For each metric computed:
  Calculate the 4-week rolling average from prior reports in /workspace/reports/.
  If this week's value is more than 2 standard deviations from the rolling average,
  add the metric to an 'Anomalies' section at the top of the report.

  Format each anomaly as:
  - METRIC NAME: current value vs 4-week avg (±N%); direction (up/down); context if available

If no anomalies: write 'No anomalies this period' in the Anomalies section.
Do not omit the section even if empty — a blank section signals the check ran.
Include the Anomalies section even when empty. A missing section is indistinguishable from a skipped check — an empty section with 'No anomalies this period' proves the logic ran.
Multi-Source Report Composition
Some recurring reports need data from multiple connectors that serve different parts of the report. Fetch each source for its relevant section rather than fetching everything upfront.

## Weekly Business Digest

### Section 1: Revenue (Google Analytics connector)
Fetch revenue, transactions, and avg order value for last 7 days.
Compute WoW change vs /workspace/reports/weekly-digest-latest.md.

### Section 2: Support Health (Jira connector)
Fetch: open ticket count by priority, tickets created this week, tickets resolved this week.
Flag: any P1 or P2 ticket open > 3 days.

### Section 3: Team Activity (Slack connector)
Fetch: messages from #product, #engineering, #sales tagged with 'shipped' or 'launched'.
Extract: features shipped, decisions made (look for message patterns like 'we decided' or 'going with').

### Section 4: Upcoming (from workspace)
Read /workspace/inputs/upcoming-milestones.md (updated manually each week).
List: top 3 milestones due in next 14 days.

Compose all sections into one report.
Output to /workspace/reports/weekly-digest-{YYYY-MM-DD}.md
Overwrite /workspace/reports/weekly-digest-latest.md with this week's data only.
Distributing Report Outputs
A recurring report is only valuable if the right people read it. Cowork can distribute outputs to Slack or other connected channels as part of the same task run, eliminating a manual forwarding step.

# Distribution step (append to any report goal):
After writing the report file:

1. Extract the Summary section from the report (the first H2 section).
2. Post to Slack #ops-weekly (connector: Slack):
   Message format:
   'Weekly Ops Report ({date}): {summary text}\n\nFull report: /workspace/reports/ops-report-{date}.md'

3. Append to /workspace/logs/distribution-log.md:
   '{date}: posted to #ops-weekly, {N} chars'
Distribution Channel
Connector Required
Content to Send
Length Limit
Slack channel
Slack
Summary section or bullet list of key metrics
Keep under 800 chars for readable preview
Google Drive
Google Drive
Full report as markdown or paste to a Doc
No practical limit; Drive handles large docs
Jira ticket update
Jira
Summary posted as a comment on a tracking ticket
Jira comment supports markdown; no strict limit
Email (via integrated tool)
Depends on setup
Executive summary section only
Email-friendly length: under 400 words
Handling Report Failures Gracefully

A recurring report task may run when source data is incomplete — a connector is unavailable, a CSV was not uploaded, or a watermark file is missing. The goal should handle these cases explicitly so the failure is visible rather than silent.
Failure Type
Goal Instruction Pattern
Output
Connector returns empty data
If connector returns 0 results, write 'No data available this period' in that section
Partial report with explicit gap noted
Prior report missing (first run or gap)
If /workspace/reports/latest.md does not exist, skip WoW comparison; label as baseline run
Report without period comparison; labeled 'Baseline'
Source CSV not updated
Check file modification date; if older than 2 days, note 'Source file may be stale (last modified: {date})'
Report with stale data warning
Connector timeout
If connector call fails, write 'Connector unavailable — section skipped' and continue other sections
Partial report; run log notes the failure
Iterating on Report Design

Recurring reports evolve over time as audiences change and requirements shift. The workspace-based design makes iteration safe: modify the goal text for the next scheduled run, and the change applies going forward without affecting the historical archive.
Add a new section: insert the data fetch and output instructions for the new section into the goal; prior archived reports will not have the section (intentional — historical fidelity matters)
Change the format: update the template file in /workspace/templates/ rather than editing the goal; the next run picks up the new template automatically

Remove a section: delete the fetch and output instructions from the goal; the latest.md baseline file will still have the old section on its next read — instruct the engine to ignore unrecognized sections from the prior baseline
Change the cadence: update the schedule frequency in the Cowork project settings; no change to the goal is needed
Test a modified goal: run the task manually against the current workspace state before the next scheduled cycle; verify the output looks correct before the schedule fires
Structured Rolling Window Analysis

Some recurring reports need to show a rolling window of periods rather than a single comparison point. A 4-week rolling window gives readers trend context that a single WoW delta cannot. The workspace accumulates the history that makes this possible.
Reading Prior Reports for Rolling Average

# Rolling window pattern — add to any metric report goal:

Before computing this week's metrics:
1. List all files matching /workspace/reports/weekly-revenue-*.md
2. Read the 4 most recent (by filename date, descending)
3. From each, extract the 'Total Revenue' line
4. Compute: rolling 4-week average = sum of 4 values / 4

Include in the Metrics section:
| Metric | This Week | Last Week | 4-Week Avg | vs 4-Week Avg |

If fewer than 4 prior reports exist, compute average from what is available;
note 'Avg based on N weeks' in the table header.
Trend Classification
Instead of just showing a number, classify the trend so readers can immediately see direction without interpreting the delta themselves.

# Trend classification instructions (add to goal):

For each metric in the report:
  Compare to the 4-week rolling average.
  Classify:
    UP_SIGNIFICANT   if > +10% from 4-week avg
    UP_MODERATE      if +3% to +10%
    STABLE           if within -3% to +3%
    DOWN_MODERATE    if -3% to -10%
    DOWN_SIGNIFICANT if < -10%

  Display trend in the table with an arrow indicator:
    UP_SIGNIFICANT   → use symbol (↑↑)
    UP_MODERATE      → (↑)
    STABLE           → (→)
    DOWN_MODERATE    → (↓)
    DOWN_SIGNIFICANT → (↓↓)
Audience-Calibrated Report Variants

The same underlying data may need different reports for different audiences. A raw metrics table suits an analyst; a narrative summary suits an executive. Writing one goal per audience variant is cleaner than one goal that tries to serve both.
Audience
Format
Length
Key Differences
Operations team
Metrics table + anomaly flags + ticket list
600-900 words
Detailed, tactical, includes every metric
Team lead
Summary bullets + WoW change + action items
300-500 words
Highlights only, omits stable metrics
Executive / board
Narrative paragraph + 3-5 key numbers
150-250 words
No tables, plain language, outcome-focused
External stakeholders
Curated highlights only, no internal metrics
100-200 words
Remove anything sensitive; focus on outcomes

# Two goals for the same weekly data, different audiences:

# Goal A — ops team report
Goal: produce a full weekly ops metrics report with all ticket counts,
resolution rates, SLA performance, and anomaly flags.
Format: tables and bullet lists. All metrics included.
Output: /workspace/reports/ops-full-{YYYY-MM-DD}.md

# Goal B — exec summary (runs after Goal A)
Read /workspace/reports/ops-full-{YYYY-MM-DD}.md.
Extract only: total tickets opened, resolution rate, and the top 2 anomalies flagged.
Write a 3-sentence narrative summary suitable for an exec update.
Output: /workspace/reports/ops-exec-summary-{YYYY-MM-DD}.md
Report Quality Checklist
Before relying on a recurring report workflow, verify it meets these quality criteria on the first few runs. Problems caught early prevent silent bad data from accumulating.
Check
Verify
Fix if Failed
Run log is written
run-log.md has a new entry after each run
Add append-to-run-log instruction at the end of the goal
Archive file created
Date-stamped file exists in /workspace/reports/ after each run
Add write-archive instruction to the goal
First-run handling
Report runs correctly when no prior files exist
Test manually by deleting /workspace/state/ and /workspace/reports/latest.md, then re-running
Empty data handling
Report notes absence explicitly rather than generating 'zero' rows silently
Add 'if no results, write: No data this period' instruction for each connector call
WoW comparison correct
Deltas match manually-computed values from the prior report
Spot-check two data points by reading the raw output files
Anomaly section present
Anomaly section appears even when empty
Make the section mandatory in the goal, not conditional
Summary
Recurring reports use scheduled tasks with goals that stay constant while the data changes each run via connectors and workspace files.
Three design patterns: stateless snapshot (current state only), period-over-period comparison (reads prior output for WoW deltas), and incremental accumulation (appends new records from a watermark).
Store report templates in /workspace/templates/ for consistent formatting across runs; instruct the engine to fill in template placeholders rather than inventing structure.
Build anomaly detection into the goal so the report flags unexpected values automatically — include the anomaly section even when empty to confirm the check ran.
Distribute outputs to Slack or other connectors as part of the same task run to eliminate manual forwarding steps.
Handle failures explicitly in the goal: empty connector data, missing prior reports, and stale files should produce partial outputs with clear notes rather than errors or silent gaps.
Iterate safely by modifying the goal text between runs; historical archives in /workspace/ preserve prior report format unchanged.
